coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis and ozonation of carbon dots using mango pulps for dual-mode colorimetry and ratiometric fluorescence detection of sarcosine
coredata.dc:creator: Qin, Weidong
Tian, Hongyuan
Meng, Zhao
Tang, Zhanqiu
Wang, Junhua
Wu, Zhenglong
coredata.dc:description: We synthesized excitation-dependent fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) through a one-pot hydrothermal process using mango pulps as precursors and discovered that the ozone-treated CDs (O3-CDs) could stabilize the diimine form of oxidized 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (diimine oxTMB). A dual-mode colorimetry and ratiometric fluorescence method using O3-CDs and TMB as probes was developed for detecting sarcosine. During measurement, sarcosine was selectively oxidized by sarcosine oxidase, and the resulting hydrogen peroxide was catalyzed by horseradish peroxidase to convert TMB into a blue-colored oxidized product. Under acidic conditions, this product was further oxidized to diimine oxTMB, which exhibited an absorbance band at 450 nm. Due to the inner filter effect, the fluorescence emission of O3-CDs at 450 nm was significantly quenched by the diimine oxTMB, while the emission at 550 nm was utilized as a reference signal. The dual-mode method selectively detected sarcosine, with detection limits of 0.034 µM and 0.096 µM for colorimetry and ratiometric fluorescence detection, respectively. The ratiometric fluorescence sensing strategy provided a built-in correction to prevent errors originating from environmental variabilities. Moreover, the stabilization of diimine oxTMB mediated by O3-CDs improved the detection performance of the method. This study enriches the library of luminescent CDs through the rational utilization of natural biomass and introduces a novel approach to improve the analytical performance of dual-mode detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine
Sarcosine
Fluorescence
Colorimetry
Ozone treatment

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Working principle of the dual-mode detection system

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21575017 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image and particle size distribution (inset) of the pristine CDs (A); UV–vis spectra of pristine and O3-CDs (B); results of XPS measurements performed on pristine CDs (C) and O3-CDs (D); photoluminescence spectra of the pristine CDs (E) and O3-CDs (F) at different excitation wavelengths.

Time-dependent UV–vis spectra of the colorimetric TMB system (A); A + 5 μL pristine CDs (B); A + 5 μL O3-CDs (C); variation in A450 as a function of incubation time (D); schematic illustrations of the parallel packing of diimine oxTMB on the pristine CD (E) and the perpendicular alignment of diimine oxTMB on the O3-CD (F).

UV–vis spectra and images of the dual-detection assay with increasing sarcosine concentration from 0.5 to 30 μM (A); linear calibration curves of A450 and I450/I540 against sarcosine concentrations from 0.5 to 30 μM (B); normalized fluorescence spectra excited at 360 nm (C) and 500 nm (D) of the dual-detection assay; the inset in (C) shows the calibration curve based on I450/I504.
Colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescent responses of sarcosine (10 μM) and potential interfering materials (100 μM). The subscript 0 denotes the values obtained from blank.
Detection of sarcosine in urine samples by the proposed strategy a).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a)the data under items “Detected (µM)” and “Recovery (%)” are average results based on three replications at each concentration.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis and ozonation of carbon dots using mango pulps for dual-mode colorimetry and ratiometric fluorescence detection of sarcosine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Beijing Normal University, Beijing 100875, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Beijing Normal University, Beijing 100875, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analytical and Testing Center, Beijing Normal University, Beijing 100875, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Analytical and Testing Center, Beijing Normal University, Beijing 100875, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors equally contributed to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We synthesized excitation-dependent fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) through a one-pot hydrothermal process using mango pulps as precursors and discovered that the ozone-treated CDs (O3-CDs) could stabilize the diimine form of oxidized 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (diimine oxTMB). A dual-mode colorimetry and ratiometric fluorescence method using O3-CDs and TMB as probes was developed for detecting sarcosine. During measurement, sarcosine was selectively oxidized by sarcosine oxidase, and the resulting hydrogen peroxide was catalyzed by horseradish peroxidase to convert TMB into a blue-colored oxidized product. Under acidic conditions, this product was further oxidized to diimine oxTMB, which exhibited an absorbance band at 450 nm. Due to the inner filter effect, the fluorescence emission of O3-CDs at 450 nm was significantly quenched by the diimine oxTMB, while the emission at 550 nm was utilized as a reference signal. The dual-mode method selectively detected sarcosine, with detection limits of 0.034 µM and 0.096 µM for colorimetry and ratiometric fluorescence detection, respectively. The ratiometric fluorescence sensing strategy provided a built-in correction to prevent errors originating from environmental variabilities. Moreover, the stabilization of diimine oxTMB mediated by O3-CDs improved the detection performance of the method. This study enriches the library of luminescent CDs through the rational utilization of natural biomass and introduces a novel approach to improve the analytical performance of dual-mode detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Sarcosine (N-methylglycine), a metabolic intermediary of glycine in the human body, has been identified as a reliable biomarker for prostate cancer.In clinical labs, sarcosine can be detected using standard kits that employ a classical oxidase-linked assay in either colorimetric or fluorescence mode [1]. On the other hand, researchers have developed various methods to detect sarcosine based on different techniques, including liquid chromatography, capillary electrophoresis, photoelectrochemistry, amperometry, reflectometric interference spectroscopy, impedance-based biomimetic detection, colorimetry, and fluorimetry [2–4]. However, detecting sarcosine is still challenging due to its low concentration in the human body and the complex composition of sample matrices. Therefore, developing new methods for the sensitive and accurate detection of sarcosine is still highly needed.

Dual-mode detection is an effective strategy for precise diagnosis. It combines two mutually independent sensing methods, which can verify each other to minimize false results and thereby improve the reliability of measurements [5–9]. In this context, it is advantageous over conventional detection modes that are based on a single signal. However, to achieve reliable and accurate results, the sensing system must be carefully designed to ensure stable signals are obtained from both detection channels.

3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) is a widely used redox and chromogenic substrate for dual-mode detection. During the measurement, it undergoes oxidation, resulting in increased absorption in the visible region [10–13]. However, researchers have found that the oxidized TMB (oxTMB) product, whether in the form of a charge transfer complex or a diimine, is unstable [14,15], which might seriously affect the accuracy of detection. Several research works have been conducted to address this problem. For instance, Li et al. stabilized oxTMB by using micelles formed by combinations of alkylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate ionic liquids and sodium dodecyl sulfate [16]. Given the extensive use of oxTMBs in analytical sciences, more efforts should be directed toward enhancing their stability in various scenarios.

Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional nanoparticles with diameters less than 10 nm. Due to quantum confinement and surface/edge state effects, CDs possess appealing physical, chemical, and biological properties, and have been widely used in various fields, such as fluorescence measurement, bioimaging, photocatalysis, and therapy in the past decade [17–26]. Generally, CDs have core–shell structures with abundant surface functional groups, including polyaromatic, carboxyl, hydroxyl, and epoxide moieties. As photoluminescent probes for dual-mode detection, CDs tend to interact with other probes, which can significantly impact the analytical performance. For example, TMB can be adsorbed onto the surface of carbon nanomaterials [27], resulting in a significant change in its UV–vis absorbance [28]. On the other hand, the surface properties of CDs have been modified in many studies through post-synthesis treatments, such as oxidation, acidification, and covalent functionalization. However, little attention has been focused on treating CDs to improve detectability in dual-mode analysis.

We recently synthesized fluorescent CDs with excitation-dependent emission properties using mango pulps as precursors through a one-pot hydrothermal synthesis protocol, followed by ozone treatment. Interestingly, the ozone-treated CDs (O3-CDs) could prevent the fading of the colorimetric probe diimine oxTMB. Inspired by the published works and our observations, we propose a dual-mode colorimetry and ratiometric fluorescence system for the simple and rapid detection of sarcosine using O3-CDs and TMB as probes. The method offers significantly low detection limits, excellent linearity, and good selectivity. The mutual verification of the strategy enhances the reliability and accuracy of the measurement. More importantly, the unique properties of O3-CDs offer enhanced analytical performance.

Synthesizing fluorescent carbon dots using natural biomass materials as carbon sources is a cost-effective and eco-friendly strategy that has received widespread attention in recent years. Mango (Mangifera indica L.) is commonly used as biomass for preparing CDs. However, despite the extensive body of literature on the use of mango peels as precursors, there are limited reports on the utilization of mango pulps as carbon sources for CDs [29]. This work contributes to our understanding of environmentally friendly synthesis and treatment of luminescent carbon dots to improve the analytical performance of dual-mode detection.

Sarcosine oxidase (SOx) and sodium chloride were purchased from Mecklin Biochemical (Shanghai, China). Sarcosine, 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), and horseradish peroxidase (HRP, >300 U mg−1) were obtained from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Sulfuric acid, disodium hydrogen phosphate, sodium dihydrogen phosphate, hydrochloric acid, and potassium chloride were products of Beijing Reagent Company (Beijing, China). Valine, phenylalanine, and alanine were purchased from Biodee Biotechnology (Beijing, China). Isoleucine, proline, aspartic acid, and methionine were purchased from Ruji Biotechnology (Shanghai, China). Glutamic acid was purchased from Dingguo (Beijing, China). Phosphotungstic acid was purchased from Bide PharmaTech (Shanghai, China). All reagents used in the experiment were of analytical reagent grade and were used without undergoing any purification treatment.

Mangoes were peeled, and the seeds were removed. The pulps were cut into cubes with a side length of approximately 2 mm and dried in a hot-air oven at 70 °C until reaching a constant weight. For the one-pot hydrothermal synthesis of carbon dots, 15.0 g of mango pulp and 10 mL of triple-distilled water were transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (Xi'an Gauge Instrument, Shaanxi, China), which was placed in an oven at 170 °C for 3 h. After cooling to room temperature, the product was filtered using gauze, centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 10 min to remove the precipitants, and finally passed through a 0.45 μm membrane (Jiuding High Tech, Beijing, China). The filtrate was subjected to rotary evaporation to remove the massive water and then placed in a 70 °C oven for 8 h to obtain the crude CD product.

The crude product was dispersed in 1.0 mL of triple-distilled water to a concentration of 8.0 mg mL−1. This solution was then fractionated using ultrafiltration membranes (Millipore, MA, USA). The fraction of CDs with nominal molecular weights ranging from 3 kDa to 50 kDa was collected (referred to as pristine CDs) and then re-dispersed in 1.0 mL of triple-distilled water for further use.

Ozone was generated by passing air through a GN-S2S generator (Igenu, Anhui, China). Through a capillary tube measuring 7 cm × 150 μm, the ozone was introduced at a flow rate of 4.8 mL min−1 into a 500 µL solution of CDs (8.0 mg mL−1), which was placed in a water bath maintained at a constant temperature of 70 °C. After 1.5 h, the product was fractionated using ultrafiltration. The ozone-treated CDs with nominal molecular weights ranging from 3 kDa to 50 kDa were collected (referred to as O3-CDs) and then re-dispersed in 500 µL of triple-distilled water for further use.

Fluorescence measurements were conducted using an FS5 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments, Livingston, UK), and fluorescence decay experiments were carried out using an FLS980 spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments). The UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded using a UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using an ESCSLAB 250XI X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Leicestershire, UK) equipped with a monochromatized Al-Ka source operated at a power of 2721 eV.

To characterize the morphology of the pristine carbon dots, the CD solution was dropcast onto a carbon-coated copper grid and dried at room temperature for 10 min. Afterward, the sample was negatively stained with 1 % phosphotungstic acid and dried at 60 °C overnight. The grid was observed on a Talos F200S transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI, Oregon, USA) operated at 200 kV.

The typical detection procedures were as follows: 150 μL of sample or standard solution was mixed with 20 μL of 20 U mL−1 SOx solution and incubated at 37 ℃ for 10 min. After cooling to room temperature, 10 μL of 5 mM TMB solution and 5 μL of 30 U mL−1 HRP solution were added. The mixture was incubated at room temperature in the dark for 5 min, followed by the addition of 20 μL of 5 M HCl and 5 μL of O3-CD solution. After incubation for an additional 5 min, UV–vis measurements were performed over a wavelength range of 200–800 nm. In addition, the relative peak height of absorbance at 450 nm (A450), obtained through baseline correction, was used for colorimetric quantification. The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded at excitation wavelengths of 360 nm and 500 nm. The ratio of the emission intensities at 450 nm and 550 nm, I450/I550, was used for quantification.

The urine sample from a healthy volunteer was filtered through a 0.45-μm membrane and then centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 20 min. Thereafter, 200 μL of the supernatant was collected and diluted fivefold with 0.1 M NaOH. The solution was placed in a 70 °C water bath and bubbled with ozone at a constant rate of 4.8 mL min−1. The resulting solution was adjusted to pH 7.4 using 3 M phosphoric acid, spiked with sarcosine to the desired concentration, and diluted to a final volume of 5.0 mL with triple-distilled water.

Mango pulps mainly contain carbohydrates, cellulose, lipids, and proteins [30]. During the hydrothermal process, these components underwent hydrolysis and aromatization, resulting in the formation of core/shell-structured CDs covered by polyaromatic carbons and oxygen-containing functional groups such as epoxide (C–O–C), hydroxyl (C–OH), carbonyl (C=O), and carboxyl (O–C=O) [31]. Moreover, ozone treatment might cause the transformation of polyaromatic and O-functional groups [32]. In this experiment, TEM, UV–vis, XPS, and fluorescence spectroscopy were employed to investigate the properties of the CDs as well as the mechanisms underlying their transformation during the preparation and ozone treatment process.

According to the TEM study, the as-synthesized CDs displayed a spherical morphology (Fig. 1 A). The typical size ranged from 4 to 11 nm, with an average diameter of 7.5 nm (Fig. 1A, inset). The UV–vis spectrum of the pristine CDs depicted an obvious absorbance band centered at 283 nm (Fig. 1B), which was attributed to the π → π* transition of sp2 polyaromatic carbons [33]. After ozone treatment, the peak was significantly reduced, and a weak absorption at 249 nm corresponding to sp2 carbons with a decreased degree of conjugation was observed. The results suggest that the ozone treatment caused damage to the polyaromatic structures on the pristine CD surfaces.

The XPS spectra of C1s (Fig. 1, Panels C and D) and the data on relative contents (Table S1) were used to evaluate the effect of ozonation on the chemical properties of CDs. The notes C1-C4 correspond to C-C and/or C-H (C1), C-O (C2), C=O and/or O-C-O (C3), and O-C=O (C4), respectively. The surface content of C1 increased from 33.3 % to 39.3 % (Table S1). In contrast, the surface content of C2 decreased from 51.2 % to 46.5 %, and the surface content of O1 decreased from 42.0 % to 36.9 % after ozonation (Table S1, Fig. S1, and Fig. S2). The results generally agree with previous observations [34]. Under alkaline conditions, ozone reacted with hydroxide ions, producing hydroxyl radicals (·OH). The radicals could then initiate the oxidation of hydroxyl groups that were present in carbohydrate and cellulose moieties on the carbon dots. The slight increase in C3 content from 12.6 % to 13.4 % suggests that a small portion of the surface hydroxyl groups of CDs were oxidized to C=O or O-C-O [35]. On the other hand, more hydroxyl groups underwent oxidation to form carbonyl (keto and aldehyde) groups, which then underwent alkali-induced β-alkoxy elimination reactions [36] and ultimately detached from the CDs [34]. The mechanism is supported by the decreased oxygen content and the increased C1 content (Table S1). Moreover, the content of C4, which represents the number of carboxyl groups on the CD surface, decreased, suggesting that the extensive ozonation treatment resulted in the decomposition of oxygen-containing functional groups [37].

It was found that the CDs exhibited an excitation-dependent luminescence behavior, which was consistent with the observations reported in the literature [38–40]. When the excitation wavelength changed from 300 to 500 nm, the corresponding maximum emission wavelength shifted to longer wavelengths, from 400 nm to 550 nm. Moreover, the luminescence between 440 and 500 nm (Fig. 1, E and F), which corresponds to the blue color in the visible region, is notably stronger. The blue-colored emission of the CDs reveals that the fluorescence was mainly attributed to intrinsic state emission, which is often determined by quantum size, zigzag edge sites, or recombination of localized electron-hole pairs [41]. Further, ozone treatment of the CDs did not cause significant changes in the profiles of the fluorescence spectra; only a slight red-shift of emission wavelengths ranging from 1 to 5 nm was observed (Table S2). However, the emission intensities were significantly enhanced. The results suggest that, after the treatment, the core sizes of the CDs did not change significantly, while changes in the capping functional groups led to an increase in luminescence intensity.

The above characterization experiments indicate that the carbon dots prepared in this study consist of carbonized cores and capping functional groups. Based on the structures and properties revealed by the aforementioned studies, the CDs can be classified as polymer carbon dots (PCDs) [42]. Ozone treatment significantly alters the chemical properties of the CD surfaces, resulting in improved photoluminescence emission efficiency, which is beneficial for sensitive detection. Additionally, due to their continuous excitation-dependent emission features, CDs have a wide range of applications in detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Working principle of the dual-mode detection system
ce:section.ce:para:
The catalytic oxidation of sarcosine by the enzyme SOx can be described by the following equation [43]:
(1)
S a r c o s i n e +
O 2
+
H 2
O
→
G l y c i n e + F o r m a l d e h y d e +
H 2
O 2
As shown in Scheme 1 , the intermediate product H2O2 was utilized in the catalytic oxidation of TMB by HRP, resulting in the formation of a blue cation radical product oxidized TMB (oxTMB), which subsequently formed a charge transfer complex with the parent TMB molecule. After acidifying with hydrochloric acid, the yellow product diimine form TMB (diimine oxTMB) was obtained. Diimine oxTMB exhibited a strong absorbance at 450 nm, and the relative peak height (A450) was used for colorimetric quantification.

Before the fluorescence measurements, fluorescence decay experiments were carried out on the O3-CDs to study the quenching mechanism. The results indicated that there were no distinct changes in the fluorescence lifetimes of the O3-CDs in the presence and absence of diimine oxTMB (4.20 ns versus 4.27 ns, Fig. S3), suggesting that no energy and/or electron transfer occurred between the two probes. Therefore, the inner-filter effect-initiated quenching was chosen for fluorescence quantification. Specifically, the fluorescence emission of O3-CDs at 450 nm (I450), which was remarkably quenched by diimine oxTMB, served as the reporter signal. For accurate analysis, the emission of O3-CDs at 550 nm (I550) was employed as the reference signal. Thus, ratiometric fluorescence sensing was established as the second channel for sarcosine detection.

Interaction between probes is a common phenomenon in dual-mode detection. In this experiment, we investigated the effect of the interaction between CDs and diimine oxTMB on the stability of the colorimetric probe using UV–vis spectroscopy. The apparent absorbance for diimine oxTMB notably decreased in the absence of CDs (Fig. 2 A), and the corresponding relative peak height A450 diminished from 0.170 at 0 min to 0.167 at 10 min (Fig. 2D). The results suggest that the probe faded. Introducing 5 µL of pristine CDs to the colorimetric detection system resulted in a more stable apparent absorbance at 450 nm (Fig. 2B), but it caused the diimine oxTMB product to fade more quickly, as evidenced by the diminished A450 from 0.140 to 0.124 (Fig. 2D). However, in the presence of 5 µL of ozone-treated CDs, the apparent absorbance at 450 nm was the most stable (Fig. 2C), with the A450 values fluctuating between 0.157 and 0.158 (Fig. 2D), which could be considered constant within experimental error. The results suggest that the chromogenic product diimine oxTMB remained unchanged during the observation.

In the SOx/HRP/TMB system, TMB was catalytically oxidized to a mixture of intermediates, e.g., cation radical, charge-transfer complex, diimine oxTMB, and nitrenium-form oxTMB, which were in equilibrium with each other [44]. According to the reaction protocol, the dominant product changed from the charge transfer complex (blue) to diimine oxTMB (yellow) upon the introduction of hydrochloric acid. In acidic buffer, HRP drove the reaction slowly toward the formation of the final product, azo-benzidine (azo dimer) [45], which had a lower absorbance at 450 nm compared to diimine oxTMB.

The diimine oxTMB molecule contains a large conjugated π-bond system. Therefore, strong π–π interactions between diimine oxTMB and pristine CDs might occur, leading to the parallel packing of the diimine oxTMB molecules on the CD surface (as schematically illustrated in Fig. 2E). Such packing orientation resulted in a significant decrease in absorbance [28], as indicated by the lowest absorbance at 0 min (Fig. 2D). In addition, mango pulp contains various reductive polyphenolic and thiol compounds, including mangiferin, gallic acid, gallotannin, quercetin, 3-methylbut-2-ene-1-thiol, 4-mercapto-4-methylpentan-2-ol, and 4-mercapto-4-methylpentan-2-one [46,47]. These compounds could potentially be incorporated into or attached to the surfaces of pristine CDs during the synthesis process, thereby inhibiting the oxidation of TMBs during measurement. The parallel packing orientation and the presence of reductive components resulted in a rapid decrease in A450, as evidenced by the steep slope of the declining trend (Fig. 2D).

O3 has a high oxidation efficiency (E0 = 2.08 V) [32], which allows it to oxidize the C-OH and C=O groups on nanomaterial surfaces [48]. Moreover, in an alkaline solution, O3 forms various reactive oxygen species, among which the hydroxyl radical (·OH) possesses a higher oxidation potential than O3 [49]. Thus, O3 and ·OH may both oxidize the surface functional groups of CDs during the treatment.

The O3 treatment damaged the surface graphitic structures of the pristine CDs, resulting in a decrease in the π-electron density of the nanostructure surface, thereby alleviating the adsorption of diimine oxTMBs. The mechanism is supported by the higher absorbance value at 0 min in the presence of O3-CDs compared to pristine CDs (Fig. 2D). Moreover, the ozone treatment oxidized the reductive components of the pristine CDs, thereby preventing the reduction of diimine oxTMBs. Furthermore, it is well known that strong hydrogen bonding can be established between benzene amine groups and carbonyl groups [50]. Therefore, hydrogen bonds are formed between the electron-deficient NH groups of the diimine structures and the carbonyl groups on the O3-CDs. Notably, in contrast to the π–π stacking-initiated parallel packing of diimine oxTMBs on pristine CDs, the hydrogen bonding interaction resulted in the perpendicular alignment of diimine oxTMB molecules on the O3-CD surfaces (Fig. 2F). This orientation did not seriously influence the UV–vis absorbance of the diimine molecule. More importantly, hydrogen bonding significantly reduced the concentration of free diimine oxTMBs in the solution and consequently inhibited the dimerization reaction, leading to a stable UV–vis absorbance of diimine oxTMB.

To achieve the best performance for sarcosine detection, the experimental conditions, including buffer pH and concentrations of SOx, HRP, and hydrochloric acid, were optimized. Because colorimetric detection is based on the change in absorbance of diimine oxTMB at 450 nm and the fluorescence signal originates from the inner filter effect of diimine oxTMB at this wavelength, the absorbance change (ΔA) at 450 nm caused by the addition of 10 μM sarcosine was selected as the index for evaluation.

Buffer pH is a key factor that affects the catalytic efficiencies of SOx and HRP. In this study, the pH of the buffer was adjusted to 1.4, 3.4, 5.4, 6.0, 7.4, and 9.4. Fig. S4 indicates that the response was highest at pH 7.4, suggesting that the catalytic activities of SOx and HRP were strongest under neutral pH conditions.

SOx and HRP are enzymes involved in cascade catalytic reactions. Fig. S5 shows that the ΔA value initially increased with increasing SOx concentration, reaching a maximum at 20 U mL−1. However, further introduction of SOx resulted in a decline in the response. The case for HRP is similar. The ΔA value increases with the concentration of HRP, reaching a maximum at 30 U mL−1. Afterward, the ΔA value exhibits a slow downward trend with further addition of HRP (Fig. S6). Introducing SOx and HRP promoted the catalytic oxidation of sarcosine and TMB, respectively, resulting in an increased absorbance response. However, it has been reported that certain enzymes may induce the formation of azo dimers into nanostructures [35], thereby causing changes in the signal of the colorimetric system.

The diimine oxTMB, which is the two-electron oxidation product, served as the signal reporter for colorimetric detection. Therefore, its yield plays a crucial role in determining the sensitivity of detection. Generally, an acidic environment is required to facilitate the conversion of the charge transfer complex to diimine oxTMB [51]. Fig. S7 indicates that the absorbance increased with the concentration of hydrochloric acid added, reaching its highest value at 5 mol/L.

Based on the above experiments, the optimal parameters for achieving the best detection performance were as follows: buffer pH = 7.4, SOx concentration = 20 U mL−1, HRP concentration = 30 U mL−1, and hydrochloric acid concentration = 5 M.

As depicted in Fig. 2A, the absorbance of the colorimetric TMB system decayed quickly from 0 to 10 min, implying that a considerable amount of azo dimers were generated at the time of measurement. Moreover, as discussed in Section 3.4, the presence of SOx and HRP might initiate the assembly of azo dimers into nanostructures, resulting in variations in the baseline of the colorimetric system. For this reason, the relative absorbance obtained after baseline correction (referred to as A450) was used for quantification.

Taking into consideration the light scattering effect of the azo dimer-based nanostructures, the fluorescence signal from CDs (I450) should be corrected to ensure accurate quantification. Our results indicated that SOx produced a weak luminescent signal at 504 nm in an acidic solution when excited at a wavelength of 360 nm. Furthermore, the addition of HRP significantly enhanced the emission (Fig. S8). At first, we attempted to use the fluorescence emission intensity (I504) originating from SOx as the reference signal, but the linearity was unsatisfactory (inset, Fig. 3 C). Then, taking advantage of the excitation-dependent emission property of the CDs, we selected the emission intensity at 550 nm (I550) from the same luminescent CD probe as the reference and found that the regression of I450/I550 versus sarcosine concentration yielded a good linear relationship (Fig. 3B).

Utilizing the developed calibration approaches, we investigated the sensitivity of the system in detecting sarcosine under optimal conditions. As shown in Fig. 3A, A450 gradually increased with the sarcosine concentration ranging from 0.5 to 30 µM, exhibiting good linearity (R2 = 0.9996, Fig. 3B). The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 0.034 µM using the equation LOD = 3σ/S, where σ represents the standard deviation of the absorbance of the blank (n = 11), and S represents the slope of the calibration plot. Furthermore, the fluorescence spectra were recorded at excitation wavelengths of 360 nm and 500 nm using various concentrations of sarcosine. The ratio of the corresponding emission intensities at 450 nm and 540 nm, I450/I550, decreased progressively as the concentration of sarcosine increased from 0.5 to 30 µM (R2 = 0.9969, Fig. 3B), with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.096 µM. Table S3 indicates that the LOD values and the linear dynamic range of the dual-mode detection in this study are comparable to or even better than those of recently reported methods, including electrochemistry, colorimetry, fluorescence, and capillary electrophoresis.

Several factors may contribute to the good performance of the method. Unlike conventional CD-based fluorescence detection relying on a single wavelength, our method utilizes built-in correction through ratiometric fluorescence sensing, which helps prevent errors that may arise from the heterogeneous distribution of probes and environmental variations, such as fluctuations in the excitation source and light scattering caused by coexisting nanostructures. More importantly, the fluorescent probe O3-CDs could stabilize the colorimetric probe diimine oxTMB, thereby enhancing the accuracy and reproducibility of the dual-detection method.

Although sarcosine was detected based on cascade enzyme-catalyzed reactions, which were beneficial for enhancing specificity, several steps of the detection process, i.e., formation of diimine oxTMB, formation of oxTMB-based nanomaterials, and analyte-CD interactions, were susceptible to interference, leading to changes in the signal. It was reported that SOx has a slight catalytic activity toward proline [52], which could bring about inaccuracies in the absorbance measurements. Moreover, amino acid molecules containing a valine moiety can bind strongly with SOx [53], which could potentially impact the formation of azo dimer-based nanomaterials mediated by SOx. Taking into account these influences, proline and valine, along with other potentially interfering amino acids such as isoleucine, phenylalanine, glutamic acid, aspartic acid, and methionine, were selected to investigate the selectivity of the method. The concentration of each interfering agent was ten times the concentration of sarcosine. Fig. 4 indicates that the signal increased significantly in the presence of sarcosine compared to the interfering substances, suggesting the good selectivity of the dual-mode sensing strategy.

To investigate the potential applications in real samples, the proposed strategy was further employed to detect sarcosine in fortified human urine samples. Table 1 indicates that for spiking levels of 1.25–10 µM (please refer to Table S4 for the original data of the measurements), the average recoveries of sarcosine range from 85.9 % to 115 %, with relative standard deviations (RSDs) ranging from 0.990 % to 5.90 % for colorimetric detection. The corresponding values for ratiometric fluorescence detection are 102–117 % and 2.62–3.93 %. The method demonstrates satisfactory recoveries, good repeatability, and very low LODs, indicating that this dual-detection platform holds potential for sarcosine testing in clinical diagnosis.

A facile and eco-friendly method was developed for synthesizing CDs with unique properties through a one-step hydrothermal reaction using mango pulp, followed by ozone treatment. A dual-detection platform composed of O3-CDs and TMB probes was developed for the detection of sarcosine. Ozone treatment deactivated the reductive components in the biomass-based CDs, making them suitable as probes for the products in redox reactions. Moreover, it was found that the O3-CDs could stabilize diimine oxTMB, which was beneficial for achieving high sensitivity and reproducibility. The ratiometric fluorescence sensing offered a built-in correction to prevent errors that might arise from environmental variabilities. Furthermore, since the signal outputs of the dual-detection mode were based on different detection mechanisms, this strategy was conducive to preventing false positives and significantly improving measurement accuracy. Most importantly, this study not only boosts the development of luminescent CDs through the rational use of natural biomass but also introduces a novel approach for treating CDs to improve their analytical performance.

Weidong Qin: Supervision, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Hongyuan Tian: Formal analysis, Validation. Zhao Meng: Formal analysis. Zhanqiu Tang: Validation. Junhua Wang: Validation. Zhenglong Wu: Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21575017).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.109468.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.F. Du, R.H. Liu, G. Linn, K. Zhao, Synthesis of N-substituted indole derivatives via PIFA-mediated intramolecular cyclization, Org. Lett. 8 (2006) 5919-5922. https://doi.org/10.1021/ol062288o.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Balancing redox activity allowing spectrophotometric detection of Au(I) using tetramethylbenzidine dihydrochloride
Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G.G. Jang, D.K. Roper, Balancing redox activity allowing spectrophotometric detection of Au(I) using tetramethylbenzidine dihydrochloride, Anal. Chem. 83 (2011) 1836-1842. https://doi.org/10.1021/ac102668q.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Alteration of l-proline oxidase activity of sarcosine oxidase and a structural interpretation
J. Anal. Bio-Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Nishiya, T. Kishimoto, Alteration of l-proline oxidase activity of sarcosine oxidase and a structural interpretation, J. Anal. Bio-Sci. 33 (2010) 161-166.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Altered substrate affinity of monomeric sarcosine oxidase by mutation of phenylalanine-103 or histidine-348
Int. J. Anal. Bio-Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Nishiya, Altered substrate affinity of monomeric sarcosine oxidase by mutation of phenylalanine-103 or histidine-348, Int. J. Anal. Bio-Sci. 1 (2013) 21-26.
